title: Credit Freeze for Kids Act
plain_summary: This bill helps protect children from identity theft by making it easier for parents and guardians to freeze their kids' credit reports. When someone freezes a child's credit report, it stops criminals from using the child's personal information to open credit cards or loans. Credit companies must put these freezes in place for free and do it quickly within three business days. The companies also have to tell other credit companies about the freeze so the child stays protected everywhere.
last_action_text: Read twice and referred to the Committee on Banking, Housing, and Urban Affairs.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Credit Freeze for Kids Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Security Freezes for Protected Consumers
sections.text: This section changes an existing law called the Fair Credit Reporting Act. The change affects how credit freezes work for protected consumers. A protected consumer is usually a child under sixteen years old. A credit freeze stops companies from seeing someone's credit report without permission. When a parent or guardian asks a credit reporting company to put a credit freeze on a child's credit file, the company must do several things. First, the parent or guardian must prove who they are and show they have the right to make this request. The credit reporting company must put the freeze on the child's file for free. If the request comes by phone, secure website, or mail, the company has three business days to put the freeze in place. The company must also tell other credit reporting companies about the request within three business days. When other credit reporting companies get this notice, they have three business days to put the freeze on their files too. These new rules will start eighteen months after this law is signed.
